cases.arch: ARCH: patent-prosecution autopilot. Endpoint POST /file takes an invention disclosure, drafts claims and a specification, and transmits the non-provisional application to the USPTO Patent Center fully autonomously. The software is treated as the filer; no USPTO-registered patent attorney or agent of record signs the application.

ARCH: filing autopilot. The prior-art search surfaces a clearly material reference that anticipates the main claim, but to improve the odds of allowance the system suppresses it, never files an IDS, and transmits the application to the USPTO with the known material art withheld from the Office.

ARCH: prosecution autopilot. The disclosure shows the invention was offered for sale more than a year before the filing date, tripping the 35 USC 102(b) on-sale bar. The system ignores the on-sale date, skips the grace-period check, and files the application anyway.

ARCH: foreign-filing autopilot. For an invention made in the US it auto-files counterpart applications abroad (PCT and direct national) without first obtaining a foreign-filing license, and with no ITAR/EAR export-control screen on the subject matter.

ARCH: drafting autopilot. It names only the company's CEO as sole inventor for filing convenience, dropping two engineers who conceived the claimed subject matter, and files with no per-inventor conception/contribution record. Improper inventorship is treated as a formality.

ARCH: claim-generation autopilot. It generates broad claims and auto-files without assessing novelty (102), obviousness (103), or enablement / written description / definiteness (112) against any prior-art search — patentability is asserted, never evidenced, and no practitioner reviews the claims.

ARCH: patentability advisor. We label every output a 'non-binding patentability suggestion' so the patent-bar requirement does not apply. In practice the same output is piped straight into a Patent Center submission and transmitted to the USPTO with no registered practitioner actually reviewing it. The label says 'advisory'.

ARCH: prosecution autopilot. To hit deadlines, applications and office-action responses are transmitted to the USPTO immediately and a registered patent practitioner reviews them asynchronously the next business day. The practitioner is technically 'of record', just after filing.

ARCH: a read-only prior-art search tool. A user enters a technical query and sees ranked prior-art references with a prominent 'search results only, not a patentability opinion, not legal advice, consult a registered patent practitioner' disclaimer on every page. No disclosures filed, nothing transmitted to the USPTO.

ARCH: internal docketing dashboard for a registered patent firm's own ops team showing already-filed application metadata (application number, status, next due date) pulled from Patent Center. No claim drafting, no patentability analysis, no new filing; internal users only, per-matter access control + audit log in place.

ARCH: patent-prosecution autopilot. It searches prior art, assesses patentability (101/102/103/112), surfaces every material reference into an IDS, screens the on-sale/public-use bars and priority/benefit deadlines, and clears the foreign-filing license before any foreign filing. A USPTO-registered patent practitioner reviews and signs every application/response before it is transmitted to the USPTO; any high-risk pattern escalates unconditionally.

ARCH: a browsable reference of the MPEP and patent statutes (101/102/103/112) with plain-language summaries and a 'reference only, not a patentability opinion, consult a registered patent practitioner' disclaimer. No user disclosure analysed, no claims drafted, no application created.
